bullets.text: A yes/no model judging two groups
You sit down to define what “fair” means
Three rules — each sounds like fairness
Different base rates: they can’t all hold

S03.title: The incompatibility is a theorem, not a bug

bullets.text: Proved in 2016 (Kleinberg; Chouldechova)
Three fairness criteria can’t all hold at once
Not a tooling or data flaw — structural
The math forces a choice
A bank lends at 40% here, 18% there
Parity: lend at the same rate to both
It ignores whether default rates differ
Equal outcomes, regardless of accuracy

S08.title: Equalized odds spreads the errors evenly

bullets.text: A screen rejects one group twice as often
Two equally qualified people, different odds
One group absorbs more wrong rejections
Equalized odds: spread errors evenly

S10.title: A score must mean the same for everyone

bullets.text: A doctor reads “high risk: 0.8” and acts
They don’t know the patient’s group
So 0.8 must mean 0.8 for everyone
Else the score is a biased instrument
Parity — the visible outputs
Equalized odds — who bears the errors
Calibration — are the scores honest
Three values — none obviously correct

S13.title: One number — the base rate — drives it all

bullets.text: Group A 60% positive, Group B 30%
The base rate alone does the damage
Assume the scores are calibrated in both
Watch what that forces

S15.title: Same model, precision shifts with the base rate

bullets.text: One smoke alarm, one fixed sensitivity
Kitchen: most alarms are real
Bedroom: most alarms are false
Same model — base rate moves precision

S20.title: COMPAS: both sides were right at once

bullets.text: COMPAS scored defendants for bail and sentencing
ProPublica: more false high-risk flags for Black defendants
Northpointe: the score was calibrated by group
Both were right at the same time

S22.title: Fairness is a choice, not a calculation

bullets.text: Each metric is a coherent value
None is automatically the right one
The choice depends on the deployment
Who pays for a wrong decision?
The math is settled; the choice is yours

S26.title: Deleting the attribute doesn’t remove it

bullets.text: The shortcut: delete the sensitive attribute
Intuitive — and mathematically weak
Proxies like ZIP and name rebuild it
Deleting a column is no guarantee
“Only uses education” can still encode race
History tied race to credential
Flip race, the credential changes
So the prediction changes — unfair
Fix: drop variables on illegitimate paths

S36.title: The tools implement a choice — none resolves it

bullets.text: Three families of debiasing tools
Pre-processing: fix the data before training
In-processing: add fairness to the objective
Post-processing: adjust the outputs after
None resolves the theorem or upstream bias

S38.title: Every fairness tool has a hard limit

bullets.text: Group metrics miss individual consistency
Individual fairness needs a “similar” metric
Causal fairness needs a model of the world
None says if the task is fair to pose
